mnemonics.title: INDEPENDENT Property Checklist
mnemonics.content: For X, Y independent: E[XY] = E[X]E[Y], Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y), Cov(X,Y)=0. Remember: 'Everything Factorizes' - joint = marginal × marginal.

mnemonics.title: Correlation vs Independence
mnemonics.content: Independence ⇒ Uncorrelation, but Uncorrelation ⇏ Independence. Think: 'Zero is not enough - independence is tougher!'

acronyms.usage: Describes random samples in statistics and machine learning. Enables using powerful limit theorems.
F_X(x) = P(X ≤ x). For independent variables: F_JOINT(x,y) = F_X(x) × F_Y(y).
Discrete case: P(X=x, Y=y) = P(X=x) × P(Y=y). Sum over all values gives 1.
M_X(t) = E[e^(tX)]. For independent sum: M_X+Y(t) = M_X(t) × M_Y(t). Useful for finding distributions.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine the unit square for two Uniform(0,1) variables. Independence means the joint density forms a flat surface (rectangular prism). Any horizontal slice looks like the Y-marginal, any vertical slice looks like X-marginal.
For discrete sums, think of convolution as sliding one PMF along another and adding overlapping products. Visually, it's like diagonal stripes in an (i,j) grid summing to each k = i+j.
For correlated variables, draw an elongated ellipse. For independent variables (zero covariance), the ellipse becomes a circle. For dependent but uncorrelated, the ellipse is rotated (diamond shape).

analogies.analogy: Like two different dice rolled by different people - the result of one tells you nothing about the other. They live in 'separate worlds'.

Like height and shoe size in adults - they're related (taller people typically have bigger feet), but the correlation is zero if the population is balanced. The relationship is non-linear (shoe size = f(height²) essentially).
If X and Y are independent, knowing Y is like knowing the weather in another city - it doesn't help predict X. E[X|Y] = E[X] because Y provides no information about X.
